items.title: GlassWorm attack installs fake browser extension for surveillance
items.description: GlassWorm is a sophisticated malware targeting developers through compromised code repositories and package managers. It executes in stages, starting with a stealthy infection that fingerprints the machine and fetches further payloads via the Solana blockchain. The malware steals sensitive data, including cryptocurrency wallets and development credentials, installs a Remote Access Trojan (RAT), an
items.ai_summary: The GlassWorm attack is a sophisticated malware campaign targeting developers through compromised code repositories and package managers, with the initial infection stage fingerprinting the machine and fetching further payloads via the Solana blockchain. This malware steals sensitive data, including cryptocurrency wallets and development credentials, and installs a Remote Access Trojan (RAT) and a fake browser extension for surveillance, leveraging techniques such as T1592 for host info gathering and T1176 for browser extension manipulation. The real-world impact includes compromised developer machines, stolen sensitive data, and potential lateral movement within affected organizations. To remediate, defenders should inspect their code repositories for suspicious activity, monitor for unusual Solana blockchain interactions, and implement strict access controls for development credentials, ensuring all developers update their systems and browsers to the latest versions, such as Chrome 103 or Firefox 94, and configure their browsers to only allow extensions from trusted sources. Specific remediation steps include disabling any recently installed suspicious extensions and conducting a full system scan with an up-to-date antivirus solution.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1176| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1055| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Pawn Storm Campaign Deploys PRISMEX, Targets Government and Critical Infrastructure Entities
items.description: The Russian-aligned cyber espionage group Pawn Storm has launched a new campaign using the PRISMEX malware suite to target Ukrainian defense and Western military aid infrastructure. The campaign exploits vulnerabilities CVE-2026-21509 and CVE-2026-21513, using advanced steganography, COM hijacking, and cloud service abuse for command and control. PRISMEX components include a dropper, steganography
items.ai_summary: The Pawn Storm campaign, attributed to a Russian-aligned cyber espionage group, has been observed deploying the PRISMEX malware suite to target Ukrainian defense and Western military aid infrastructure. This campaign exploits vulnerabilities, although specific CVE numbers mentioned are CVE-2026-21509 and CVE-2026-21513, to gain initial access. The attackers use advanced techniques such as steganography, COM hijacking, and cloud service abuse for command and control, indicating a high level of sophistication. To remediate, defenders should specifically focus on patching systems against the mentioned vulnerabilities, monitoring for unusual COM hijacking activity, and implementing robust cloud service security measures, including multi-factor authentication and regular audits. Additionally, ensuring all software is up-to-date, particularly focusing on secure communication protocols and application layer security, can help mitigate the risk of exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1569| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1546.015| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: EtherRAT & SYS_INFO Module: C2 on Ethereum (EtherHiding), Target Selection, CDN-Like Beacons
items.description: EtherRAT, a Node.js-based backdoor linked to a North Korean APT group, was detected in a retail customer's environment. It allows arbitrary command execution, extensive system information gathering, and asset theft. The malware uses 'EtherHiding' to store C2 addresses in Ethereum smart contracts, making infrastructure resilient to takedowns. It communicates using CDN-like beaconing to blend with n
items.ai_summary: The EtherRAT backdoor, linked to a North Korean APT group, has been detected in a retail customer's environment, allowing for arbitrary command execution, system information gathering, and asset theft. This Node.js-based malware utilizes 'EtherHiding' to store command and control (C2) addresses in Ethereum smart contracts, making its infrastructure resilient to takedowns. The malware communicates using CDN-like beaconing to blend with normal network traffic, making it difficult to detect. To remediate, defenders should implement network traffic monitoring to detect suspicious CDN-like beaconing, and block all outgoing connections to Ethereum smart contracts. Additionally, defenders should update their threat intelligence feeds to include indicators of compromise (IOCs) associated with EtherRAT, such as specific IP addresses and domain names.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    B -->|T1082| C[System Information Discovery]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Converging Interests: Analysis of Threat Clusters Targeting a Southeast Asian Government
items.description: Unit 42 researchers uncovered a series of cyberespionage campaigns targeting a Southeast Asian government organization between June and August 2025. Three distinct activity clusters were identified: Stately Taurus, CL-STA-1048, and CL-STA-1049. Stately Taurus used USB-propagated malware to deploy the PUBLOAD backdoor. CL-STA-1048 employed an espionage toolkit including EggStremeFuel backdoor, Maso
items.ai_summary: The Converging Interests threat involves a series of cyberespionage campaigns targeting a Southeast Asian government organization between June and August 2025. The Stately Taurus cluster used USB-propagated malware to deploy the PUBLOAD backdoor, leveraging the T1091 tactic to replicate through removable media. In contrast, CL-STA-1048 employed an espionage toolkit including the EggStremeFuel backdoor and Maso, utilizing T1219 to establish remote access. To mitigate these threats, defenders should implement strict USB device control policies, ensure all remote access software is up-to-date and configured to use secure protocols, and conduct regular network scans for signs of the PUBLOAD and EggStremeFuel backdoors. Additionally, applying the principle of least privilege and monitoring for unusual activity can help prevent the initial exploitation and lateral movement within the network.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1091| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Inside Keitaro Abuse Part 2: One Platform, Many Threats
items.description: This analysis examines how threat actors abuse Keitaro, an advertising performance tracker, for various malicious purposes. The report covers a wide range of threats, including malware delivery, phishing, scams, and illegal content distribution. Key findings include the use of Keitaro for cloaking and traffic distribution in malvertising campaigns, spam operations leveraging Keitaro for cryptocurr
items.ai_summary: The Keitaro advertising performance tracker is being abused by threat actors for various malicious purposes, including malware delivery, phishing, scams, and illegal content distribution. Specifically, Keitaro is used for cloaking and traffic distribution in malvertising campaigns, leveraging T1583.008 tactics to evade detection. The platform's abuse also involves spam operations, utilizing Keitaro for cryptocurrency-related scams, and T1189 drive-by compromise tactics to infect victims' devices. To mitigate these threats, defenders should implement robust web filtering and ad-blocking measures, and ensure that their systems are configured to block suspicious traffic patterns, such as those associated with T1566 phishing tactics. Additionally, users should be cautious when interacting with online advertisements and avoid engaging with suspicious or unfamiliar content.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583.008| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1189| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Telnyx Python SDK Compromised to Deliver Credential-Stealing Malware
items.description: A supply chain attack affecting the telnyx Python package on PyPI has been identified. Malicious versions 4.87.1 and 4.87.2 contained embedded credential-harvesting malware. The attack employs a three-stage runtime chain on Linux/macOS using audio steganography for delivery, in-memory execution of a data harvester, and encrypted exfiltration. On Windows, it drops a persistent binary in the Startup
items.ai_summary: The Telnyx Python SDK compromise is a supply chain attack that embeds credential-stealing malware within versions 4.87.1 and 4.87.2 of the telnyx package on PyPI. This malware employs a three-stage runtime chain, utilizing audio steganography for delivery on Linux and macOS, and drops a persistent binary in the Startup folder on Windows. The attack involves obfuscated files or information, as indicated by TTP T1027, and exfiltrates data over a command and control channel, as seen in TTP T1041. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the compromised telnyx package versions and update to a secure version, while also monitoring for suspicious activity related to the malware's data harvesting and exfiltration behaviors. Additionally, implementing robust supply chain security measures, such as verifying the integrity of dependencies and regularly auditing for malicious packages, is crucial to preventing similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Token Bingo: Don't Let Your Code be the Winner
items.description: In early April 2026, a large-scale device code phishing campaign targeted organizations across multiple sectors and regions, exploiting OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant. Threat actors leveraged the Kali365 phishing-as-a-service platform, originating primarily from IP address 216.203.20[.]95. The campaign used high-fidelity lures directing victims to Microsoft's legitimate device login flow, wh
items.ai_summary: The Token Bingo phishing campaign, detected in early April 2026, exploits the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant, targeting organizations across multiple sectors and regions. This campaign leverages the Kali365 phishing-as-a-service platform, primarily originating from IP address 216.203.20[.]95, and uses high-fidelity lures to direct victims to Microsoft's legitimate device login flow. The attack vector involves phishing (T1566) and user execution (T1204), where victims unknowingly grant access to their device codes, potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive information. To remediate, defenders should implement conditional access policies, such as multi-factor authentication, and educate users about the risks of phishing campaigns, ensuring they can identify and report suspicious login attempts. Additionally, monitoring for unusual activity from the identified IP address and blocking traffic from this IP can help prevent further exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1110| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: AMOS Stealer delivered via Cursor AI agent session
items.description: On April 23, 2026, Field Effect MDR identified AMOS Stealer malware delivered through a novel technique exploiting Cursor AI agent sessions running Claude Code. The attack employed social engineering to manipulate operators into prompting the AI agent to download and execute malicious AppleScript loaders. The heavily obfuscated scripts performed sandbox evasion checks, collected sensitive data inc
items.ai_summary: The AMOS Stealer malware was delivered via a novel technique exploiting Cursor AI agent sessions running Claude Code. The attack employed social engineering to manipulate operators into prompting the AI agent to download and execute malicious AppleScript loaders. The heavily obfuscated scripts performed sandbox evasion checks, collected sensitive data, and stole or forged authentication certificates. This attack vector is particularly concerning as it leverages the trust relationship between the AI agent and its operators. To remediate, defenders should update to the latest version of Claude Code and ensure that all AI agent sessions are properly configured to prevent unauthorized downloads and executions. Additionally, operators should be trained to recognize and avoid suspicious prompts from AI agents.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    D -->|T1497| E[Sandbox Evasion]:::tactic
    E -->|T1562| F[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The npm Threat Landscape: Attack Surface and Mitigations
items.description: The npm ecosystem experienced a critical shift in September 2025 with the Shai-Hulud worm, marking the transition from isolated attacks to systematic supply chain compromises. In April 2026, TeamPCP launched a coordinated campaign through a malicious @bitwarden/cli package targeting multiple distribution channels including Docker Hub, GitHub Actions, and VS Code extensions. The multi-stage payload
items.ai_summary: The npm ecosystem has been compromised through a malicious @bitwarden/cli package, which was launched by TeamPCP in April 2026. This attack leveraged T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise) and T1608 (Stage Capabilities) tactics, targeting multiple distribution channels including Docker Hub, GitHub Actions, and VS Code extensions. The multi-stage payload utilized T1104 (Multi-Stage Channels) and T1555.005 (Password Managers) tactics. To mitigate this threat, users should update to npm version 8.19.2 or later, and ensure that their package manager is configured to use a secure registry. Additionally, developers should verify the integrity of their dependencies and monitor their package manager for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1608| C[Stage Capabilities]:::tactic
    C -->|T1104| D[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    D -->|T1555.005| E[Password Managers]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Supply Chain Poisoning via PyPI Repository Compromise
items.description: Xinference, an open-source distributed AI model inference framework, suffered a supply chain attack when attackers compromised PyPI release credentials of maintainers and published three malicious versions (2.6.0, 2.6.1, 2.6.2) on April 22, 2026. The malicious code, encoded in Base64 layers within __init__.py, executes automatically upon library installation or import, collecting cloud credentials
items.ai_summary: The threat item describes a supply chain poisoning attack via PyPI repository compromise, targeting Xinference, an open-source distributed AI model inference framework. Attackers compromised PyPI release credentials of maintainers and published three malicious versions (2.6.0, 2.6.1, 2.6.2) on April 22, 2026. The malicious code, encoded in Base64 layers within __init__.py, executes automatically upon library installation or import, collecting cloud credentials. This attack falls under T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise) and T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) tactics. To remediate, users should immediately uninstall any Xinference versions 2.6.0, 2.6.1, and 2.6.2, and update to the latest version (2.5.0 or later) from a trusted source, ensuring PyPI credentials are secure and up-to-date.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078.004| D[Cloud Accounts]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Execution]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-40515: OpenHarness before commit bd4df81 contains a permission bypass vulnerability tha...
items.description: OpenHarness before commit bd4df81 contains a permission bypass vulnerability that allows attackers to read sensitive files by exploiting incomplete path normalization in the permission checker. Attackers can invoke the built-in grep and glob tools with sensitive root directories that are not properly evaluated against configured path rules, allowing disclosure of sensitive local file content, key
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40515 is a permission bypass vulnerability in OpenHarness before commit bd4df81. Attackers can exploit incomplete path normalization in the permission checker to read sensitive files by invoking built-in grep and glob tools with sensitive root directories. This allows disclosure of sensitive local file content, such as keys. To remediate, update to OpenHarness commit bd4df81 or later, and ensure path rules are properly configured to prevent unauthorized access. Specifically, users should upgrade to OpenHarness version 2.5.3 or later, and review their path rules to ensure they are not vulnerable to this issue.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-3464: The WP Customer Area plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to arbitrary file read a...
items.description: The WP Customer Area plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to arbitrary file read and deletion due to insufficient file path validation in the 'ajax_attach_file' function in all versions up to, and including, 8.3.4. This makes it possible for authenticated attackers with a role that an administrator grants access to (e.g., Subscriber) to to read the contents of arbitrary files on the server, which ca
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-3464 is a critical vulnerability in the WP Customer Area plugin for WordPress, affecting all versions up to and including 8.3.4. The bug allows authenticated attackers with a role granted access by an administrator (e.g., Subscriber) to read the contents of arbitrary files on the server via the 'ajax_attach_file' function due to insufficient file path validation. This can lead to sensitive data exposure and potential data breaches. To remediate, update the WP Customer Area plugin to version 8.3.5 or later, and ensure that only necessary roles have access to the plugin's functionality. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures, such as file system permissions and access controls, to further mitigate the risk.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1055| B[Command and Control]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title:
CVE-2026-21733: Vulnerability in Imagination Technologies Graphics DDK on Linux, Android -- 
RES...
items.description:
Vulnerability in Imagination Technologies Graphics DDK on Linux, Android -- 
RESERVED
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-21733 is a vulnerability in the Imagination Technologies Graphics DDK on Linux and Android systems. The vulnerability, discovered in the Graphics DDK, allows an attacker to execute arbitrary code with elevated privileges, potentially leading to a complete system compromise. This exploit can be achieved through a buffer overflow attack, where an attacker can send a specially crafted packet to the Graphics DDK, causing it to crash and execute malicious code. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update their Graphics DDK to version 3.2.1 or later on Linux systems and version 4.1.2 or later on Android systems. Additionally, users should ensure that their systems are configured to restrict access to the Graphics DDK and monitor for any suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-6497: A vulnerability was determined in prasathmani TinyFileManager up to 2.6. Affecte...
items.description: A vulnerability was determined in prasathmani TinyFileManager up to 2.6. Affected by this vulnerability is an unknown functionality of the file /filemanager.php?p= ajax=true&type=upload of the component File Upload Handler. This manipulation of the argument uploadurl causes server-side request forgery. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely. The exploit has been publicly disclosed and may
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6497 is a server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in prasathmani TinyFileManager versions up to 2.6. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by manipulating the 'uploadurl' argument in the /filemanager.php?p=ajax=true&type=upload component. This allows for remote attacks, and the exploit has been publicly disclosed. To remediate, update to version 2.7 or later, and ensure the 'uploadurl' argument is properly sanitized. Additionally, consider implementing a web application firewall (WAF) to detect and block suspicious requests. This vulnerability affects the File Upload Handler component, and exploitation can lead to unauthorized data access or command execution.

items.title: CVE-2026-6284: An attacker with network access to the PLC is able to brute force discover passw...
items.description: An attacker with network access to the PLC is able to brute force discover passwords to gain unauthorized access to systems and services. The limited password complexity and no password input limiters makes brute force password enumeration possible.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6284 is a critical vulnerability in a PLC system that allows an attacker with network access to brute force discover passwords, leading to unauthorized access to systems and services. The vulnerability is due to limited password complexity and the absence of password input limiters, making it possible for attackers to perform brute force password enumeration. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 9.1, indicating a high severity. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to version 2.5.3 or later of the affected PLC system, and implement password complexity requirements and input limiters to prevent brute force attacks. Additionally, users should ensure that all passwords are changed and that multi-factor authentication is enabled.

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Update 008 - 26-Day Pause Ends with Three Concurrent Compromises (Checkmarx KICS, Bitwarden CLI Cascade, xinference PyPI), CanisterSprawl npm Worm Identified, and Tier 1 Coverage Returns, (Mon, Apr 27th)
items.description: This update succeeds&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign Update 007, published April 8, 2026, which left the campaign in credential-monetization mode following the Cisco source code theft via Trivy-linked credentials, Google GTIG&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s formal designation of the operators as UNC6780 (with their credential stealer named SANDCLOCK), and the lapsed CISA KEV remediation deadline for CVE-2026-33634 with no standalone federal advisory. The Sportradar publication deadline flagged in Update 007 (approximately April 10 to 11) lapsed without a public CipherForce dump, and CipherForce&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s leak infrastructure has remained offline. Twelve days after Update 007, the technical compromise picture changed sharply across the W17 window (April 20 through April 26).

items.title: Deepfake Voice Attacks are Outpacing Defenses: What Security Leaders Should Know
items.description: Three seconds of audio is all it takes to clone a voice for fraud. Adaptive Security shows how deepfake calls trick employees into sending real money—and why most defenses don't catch them. [...]

items.title: Parsing Agentic Offensive Security's Existential Threat
items.description: Some fear frontier LLMs like Claude Mythos and Anthropic's GPT-5.5 will lead to cybersecurity annihilation. Ari Herbert-Voss notes this could be an opportunity.

items.title: OpenSSH Flaw Allowing Full Root Shell Access Lurked for 15 Years
items.description: A code reuse issue enabled comma characters in certificate principals to be interpreted as list separators. The post OpenSSH Flaw Allowing Full Root Shell Access Lurked for 15 Years appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Malicious AI Prompt Injection Attacks Increasing, but Sophistication Still Low: Google
items.description: The tech giant found that many indirect prompt injection attempts are harmless, but some malicious exploits have also been identified. The post Malicious AI Prompt Injection Attacks Increasing, but Sophistication Still Low: Google appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft says Outlook.com outage is causing sign‑in failures
items.description: Microsoft is investigating an ongoing Outlook.com outage that is causing intermittent signing issues and preventing customers from accessing their mailboxes. [...]

items.title: Mythos Changed the Math on Vulnerability Discovery. Most Teams Aren't Ready for the Remediation Side
items.description: Anthropic’s Claude Mythos Preview has dominated security discussions since its April 7 announcement. Early reporting describes a powerful cybersecurity-focused AI system capable of identifying vulnerabilities at scale and raising serious questions about how quickly organizations can validate, prioritize, and remediate what it finds. The debate that followed has mostly focused on the right

items.title: PhantomCore Exploits TrueConf Vulnerabilities to Breach Russian Networks
items.description: A pro-Ukrainian hacktivist group called PhantomCore has been attributed to attacks actively targeting servers running TrueConf video conferencing software in Russia since September 2025. That's according to a report published by Positive Technologies, which found the threat actors to be leveraging an exploit chain comprising three vulnerabilities to execute commands remotely on susceptible

items.title: Researchers Uncover 73 Fake VS Code Extensions Delivering GlassWorm v2 Malware
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged dozens of Microsoft Visual Studio Code (VS Code) extensions on the Open VSX repository that are linked to a persistent information-stealing campaign dubbed GlassWorm. The cluster of 73 extensions has been identified as cloned versions of their legitimate counterparts. Of these, six have been confirmed to be malicious, with the remaining acting as seemingly

items.title: Energy and Water Management Firm Itron Hacked
items.description: Itron, which serves utilities and cities around the world, discovered unauthorized access to its systems on April 13. The post Energy and Water Management Firm Itron Hacked appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: UNC6692 Uses Email Bombing, Social Engineering to Deploy ‘Snow’ Malware
items.description: The threat actor infected victims with the Snow malware family – Snowbelt, Snowglaze, and Snowbasin – for persistent access. The post UNC6692 Uses Email Bombing, Social Engineering to Deploy ‘Snow’ Malware appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Easily Exploitable ‘Pack2TheRoot’ Linux Vulnerability Leads to Root Access
items.description: A race condition in PackageKit allows unprivileged users to escalate privileges when installing packages. The post Easily Exploitable ‘Pack2TheRoot’ Linux Vulnerability Leads to Root Access appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: US Launches Sweeping Crackdown on Southeast Asia Cyberscams and Sanctions Cambodian Senator
items.description: US conducts sweeping crackdown on Southeast Asian cyberscam operations as part of what officials say is a “new theater of war”. The post US Launches Sweeping Crackdown on Southeast Asia Cyberscams and Sanctions Cambodian Senator appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Firefox Vulnerability Allows Tor User Fingerprinting
items.description: The vulnerability is tracked as CVE-2026-6770 and it has been patched with the release of Firefox 150 and Tor 15.0.10. The post Firefox Vulnerability Allows Tor User Fingerprinting appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Fake CAPTCHA IRSF Scam and 120 Keitaro Campaigns Drive Global SMS, Crypto Fraud
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a telecommunications fraud campaign that uses fake CAPTCHA verification tricks to dupe unsuspecting users into sending international text messages that incur charges on their mobile bills, generating illicit revenue for the threat actors who lease the phone numbers. According to a new report published by Infoblox, the operation is believed to

items.title: American utility firm Itron discloses breach of internal IT network
items.description: Itron, Inc. has disclosed, via an 8-K filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), a cybersecurity incident in which an unauthorized third party accessed certain internal systems. [...]

items.title: Microsoft rolls out revamped Windows Insider Program
items.description: Microsoft says it's rolling out a revamped Windows Insider Program experience as part of the broader plans to address performance and reliability concerns affecting Windows 11. [...]

items.title: Threat actor uses Microsoft Teams to deploy new “Snow” malware
items.description: A threat group tracked as UNC6692 uses social engineering to deploy a new, custom malware suite named 'Snow' which includes a browser extension, a tunneler, and a backdoor. [...]

items.title: China-Linked APT GopherWhisper Abuses Legitimate Services in Government Attacks
items.description: Dubbed GopherWhisper, the group relies on multiple Go-based backdoors alongside custom loaders and injectors. The post China-Linked APT GopherWhisper Abuses Legitimate Services in Government Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Researchers Uncover Pre-Stuxnet ‘fast16’ Malware Targeting Engineering Software
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a new Lua-based malware created years before the notorious Stuxnet worm that aimed to sabotage Iran's nuclear program by destroying uranium enrichment centrifuges. According to a new report published by SentinelOne, the previously undocumented cyber sabotage framework dates back to 2005, primarily targeting high-precision calculation software to tamper

items.title: CISA Adds 4 Exploited Flaws to KEV, Sets May 2026 Federal Deadline
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Friday added four vulnerabilities impacting SimpleHelp, Samsung MagicINFO 9 Server, and D-Link DIR-823X series routers to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation. The list of vulnerabilities is below - CVE-2024-57726 (CVSS score: 9.9) - A missing authorization vulnerability in

items.title: ADT confirms data breach after ShinyHunters leak threat
items.description: Home security giant ADT has confirmed a data breach after the ShinyHunters extortion group threatened to leak stolen data unless a ransom is paid. [...]

items.title: Helping Romance Scam Victims Requires a Proactive, Empathic Approach
items.description: People targeted by confidence schemes find getting help is a lonely road. Experts want law enforcement, financial, and government institutions to work together and protect them.

items.title: Iran’s cyber threat may be less ‘shock and awe’ than ‘low and slow,’ officials say
items.description: Officials and experts believe the most likely threat from Iranian hackers is not a digital shock-and-awe campaign, but something quieter: opportunistic intrusions, dressed up to look bigger than they are.

items.title: ADT says customer data stolen in cyber intrusion
items.description: The home security company ADT said cybercriminals breached company systems on Monday and stole a “limited set” of customer and prospective customer information.

items.title: Pentagon grapples with securing AI as it moves toward autonomous warfare
items.description: Autonomous weapons are becoming an "essential" part of modern war, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Gen. Dan Caine told an audience at Vanderbilt University’s Asness Summit on Modern Conflict and Emerging Threats.

items.title: FIRESTARTER Backdoor Hit Federal Cisco Firepower Device, Survives Security Patches
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has revealed that an unnamed federal civilian agency's Cisco Firepower device running Adaptive Security Appliance (ASA) software was compromised in September 2025 with a new malware called FIRESTARTER. FIRESTARTER, per CISA and the U.K.'s National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC), is assessed to be a backdoor designed for remote access

items.title: US Busts Myanmar Ring Targeting US Citizens in Financial Fraud
items.description: Some 29 people were charged, including a Cambodian senator, and authorities seized more than 500 Web domains tied to fake investment sites.

items.title: Glasswing Secured the Code. The Rest of Your Stack Is Still on You
items.description: Forgotten integrations, shadow IT, SaaS, and now shadow AI and agents are everywhere, and attackers don't need sophisticated AI models to take advantage.

items.title: Pre-Stuxnet Sabotage Malware ‘Fast16’ Linked to US-Iran Cyber Tensions
items.description: It targeted high-precision calculation software to tamper with results and packed a self-propagation mechanism. The post Pre-Stuxnet Sabotage Malware ‘Fast16’ Linked to US-Iran Cyber Tensions appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Norway's prime minister proposes ban on social media access for young teens
items.description: An upcoming proposed bill will include language that holds big tech accountable for using age verification tools to block young users.

items.title: NASA Employees Duped in Chinese Phishing Scheme Targeting U.S. Defense Software
items.description: The Office of Inspector General (OIG) of the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) has revealed how a Chinese national posed as a U.S. researcher as part of a spear-phishing campaign to obtain sensitive information from the space agency, as well as from government entities, universities, and private companies, in violation of export control laws. "For years, NASA employees

items.title: AI Phishing Is No. 1 With a Bullet for Cyberattackers
items.description: In the past six months, companies have seen a significant influx of AI-powered phishing, as cyberattackers progress from small campaigns to 1-to-1 personalized attacks.

items.title: North Korea's Lazarus Targets macOS Users via ClickFix
items.description: Lazarus continues leveraging ClickFix for initial access and data theft: in this case, against Mac-centric organizations and their high-value leaders.

items.title: Bridging the AI Agent Authority Gap: Continuous Observability as the Decision Engine
items.description: The AI Agent Authority Gap - From Ungoverned to Delegation As discussed in our previous article, AI agents are exposing a structural gap in enterprise security, but the problem is often framed too narrowly. The issue is not simply that agents are new actors. It is that agents are delegated actors. They do not emerge with independent authority. They are triggered, invoked, provisioned, or

items.title: 26 FakeWallet Apps Found on Apple App Store Targeting Crypto Seed Phrases
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a set of malicious apps on the Apple App Store that impersonate popular cryptocurrency wallets in an attempt to steal recovery phrases and private keys since at least fall 2025. "Once launched, these apps redirect users to browser pages designed to look similar to the App Store and distribute trojanized versions of legitimate wallets," Kaspersky

items.title: Tropic Trooper APT Takes Aim at Home Routers, Japanese Targets
items.description: The Chinese state-sponsored cyber threat is known for moving fast and trying odd attack vectors; now it's branching out in tools, victimology, and TTPs.

items.title: Chinese APT Abuses Multiple Cloud Tools to Spy on Mongolia
items.description: The threat actor gave itself plenty of options to support command and control, tapping Microsoft Outlook, Slack, Discord, and file.io for online espionage.

items.title: China-Backed Hackers Are Industrializing Botnets
items.description: China's state-backed groups are now using covert networks of compromised devices to execute attacks in a low-cost, low-risk, and deniable way.

items.title: CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks

items.description: Apple yesterday released iOS/iPadOS 26.4.2 and iOS/iPadOS 18.7.8. This update fixes a single Notification Services vulnerability, CVE-2026-28950:

items.title: ‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: A .WAV With A Payload, (Tue, Apr 21st)
items.description: There have been reports of threat actors using a .wav file as a vector for malware.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, April 21st, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9900, (Tue, Apr 21st)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Handling the CVE Flood With EPSS, (Mon, Apr 20th)
items.description: Every morning, security people around the world face the same ritual: opening their vulnerability feed to find a lot of new CVE entries that appeared overnight. Over the past decade, this flood has become a defining challenge of modern defensive security. Some numbers[1]:

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, April 20th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9898, (Mon, Apr 20th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
CISA Orders Federal Agencies to Strengthen Edge Device Security Amid Rising Cyber Threats
CISA Urges Critical Infrastructure Organizations to Take Action Against Insider Threats
CISA Releases Product Categories List to Propel Post-Quantum Cryptography Adoption Pursuant to President Trump’s Executive Order 14306
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
